Impact of Infrastructure & Socioeconomic Status on Rural Education
Independent Research | Primary Investigator
Objective: Investigated how physical infrastructure and socioeconomic status (SES) influence educational outcomes in rural

Data Analysis: Analyzed ASER data indicating that while 96.7% of children are enrolled in schools, basic infrastructure remains
a barrier, with only 61.75% of schools having accessible toilets and 11% offering transportation.

correlates with higher dropout rates due to financial constraints.
Technical Insights: Utilized Python (Seaborn) to visualize the "Digital Divide," highlighting that while platforms like DIKSHA
exist, fewer than 20% of rural schools have functional internet access.

community-based digital learning centers, and subsidized transportation.

- Completed research project titled Predicting Affordability of a Family Paying Medical and Health Care Expenses Development and Validation of a Predictive Model and qualified for regional SEFH competition.
- The project aimed to develop an applicable and reliable predictive model to predict whether a family can afford medical care based on 2021 National Survey of Childrens Health (NSCH) dataset, which can be applied by insurance companies and welfare institutions to determine appropriate insurance premiums and levels of financial assistance to different families, thus helping to improve medical expense affordability.

- Built an RL post-training pipeline to learn a paraphrasing policy that generalizes across multiple AI-text detector families while preserving semantic meaning and fluency, with a focus on ESL fairness.
- Implemented GRPO with LoRA adapters on Qwen3-4B-Instruct and a composite reward (detector-ensemble score, semantic similarity, perplexity, ESL fairness penalty); ran training and ablation studies on Tinker.

Project: Model Organisms for Privacy & Backdoor Failures in Image Classifiers
Model Organisms for Privacy & Backdoor Failures in Image Classifiers
- Implemented the membership inference framework on Fashion-MNIST: trained shadow models and an XGBoost attack classifier, plus a confidence-based baseline, under a standard threat model where the adversary only observes model outputs.
- Empirically showed that for a well-regularized CNN, both attacks collapse to near-random performance (ROC AUC 0.5), then trained DP-SGD variants to probe the privacy utility tradeoff as test accuracy falls from 90% to 65% under stronger privacy.
- Implemented a backdoor poisoning on MNIST: injecting a subtle Pikachu trigger into just 1.75% of training data yields 100% attack success with 95.69% clean accuracy, illustrating how catastrophic misbehavior can hide behind strong aggregate metrics.
